title: The illusion of skill
grounding: Survivorship bias: we only observe winners; the many who tried the same approach and failed are invisible. Lottery-ticket fallacy: treating a payoff as proof of a good strategy when the strategy might just be buying lots of tickets. Alternative histories: you can't judge a decision by its one outcome, only by the range of outcomes that could plausibly have happened if replayed many times (Taleb's example: $10M from careful dentistry and $10M from one round of Russian roulette are not the same bet, even though they spend the same).

title: Black swans & the narrative fallacy
grounding: A Black Swan has three traits: rarity (an outlier nothing in the past pointed to), extreme impact, and retrospective (not prospective) predictability. Narrative fallacy: our need to fit a convincing story to a series of facts after the outcome is known; a story built after the fact can raise confidence without raising predictive ability. Mediocristan (no single observation can move the aggregate) vs. Extremistan (a single observation can dwarf everything else). The turkey problem: no finite number of confirming observations can prove a rule, but a single disconfirming one can break it (a turkey fed for 1000 days learns nothing about Thanksgiving).

title: Probability without a spreadsheet
book: The Black Swan / Statistical Consequences of Fat Tails
grounding: Distinguish the variable x (will this event happen?) from your exposure f(x) (what happens to you if it does?). Under fat tails, small-sample statistics understate extremes, so 'what's my exposure if I'm wrong' matters more than 'what's the probability.' Ludic fallacy: mistaking the well-posed problems of games/casinos for the open-ended uncertainty of real life, where nobody wrote down the full list of possible outcomes in advance.

title: Antifragility
grounding: Fragile things break under stress (a teacup); robust things stay the same (a rock); antifragile things get stronger from moderate, irregular, survivable stress (a muscle). Quote: 'Antifragility is beyond resilience or robustness. The resilient resists shocks and stays the same; the antifragile gets better.' The mechanism is convexity: an antifragile system's response curves upward with stress dose. Dose matters — benefits only apply within a survivable range; more stress is not always better.

title: Via negativa & the barbell strategy
grounding: Via negativa: durable improvement usually comes from removing a harmful exposure, not adding a promising one, since removal rarely creates new hidden fragilities the way addition does. Barbell strategy: split exposure between extreme safety (large portion, cannot be wiped out) and extreme, capped-downside speculation (small portion, open upside) — avoid the 'medium risk' middle where a single bad tail event can ruin you. Iatrogenics: harm caused by an intervention whose stated purpose was to help ('caused by the healer'); naive interventionism is the urge to 'fix' something that would have been fine left alone.

title: Optionality & convexity
grounding: Optionality: the right, but not the obligation, to take an upside if conditions are favorable, while downside stays small and known. Convexity: a payoff shape where being wrong costs little and being right pays disproportionately — you don't need to predict correctly more than half the time to come out ahead. Tinkering (cheap trial-and-error) beats top-down planning in complex systems as long as each attempt is convex: capped cost of failure, open-ended payoff of success.

title: Skin in the game
grounding: Fairness and trustworthy advice come from symmetry: whoever gets the upside of a decision should also bear its downside. When that breaks, you get fragility of the whole system, not just an ethical complaint. The 'Intellectual Yet Idiot' (IYI): credentialed people confident enough to prescribe what others should do, without ever paying a cost when wrong. Black Swan ethical problem: because Black Swans are nonrepeatable, there's an asymmetry between the rewards of those who visibly cure a disaster and those who invisibly prevent one.

title: The Lindy effect
grounding: For non-perishable things (ideas, books, technologies, institutions), the longer something has already survived, the longer its expected remaining lifespan — the opposite of how a mortal thing ages. Age is evidence of having been tested against a long, uncontrolled series of real conditions. Quote: 'If a book has been in print for forty years, I can expect it to be in print for another forty years... if it survives another decade, then it will be expected to be in print another fifty years.' The heuristic can be misapplied exactly like the turkey problem — survival so far is not proof of permanent robustness.

title: The minority rule
grounding: A small, rigid, uncompromising minority (often 3-4%) can force an entire flexible majority to comply, because the minority will never deviate while the majority has no strong preference either way. Quote: 'A Kosher (or halal) eater will never eat nonkosher (or nonhalal) food, but a nonkosher eater isn't banned from eating kosher.' The minority's rigidity usually comes from a real stake (religious commitment, health risk, moral line) — their skin in the game is what makes them powerful out of proportion to their numbers.

title: Domain dependence
grounding: Domain dependence is failing to transfer an insight or pattern from the context where it was learned to a different context where it applies just as well (a surgeon who checks vitals rigorously but invests on a hot tip). It's invisible from the inside — it feels like confident judgment, not ignorance, because you're skilled in the domain you're judging from.

title: The Bed of Procrustes
grounding: This book is a collection of standalone aphorisms, not a sustained argument — each one deliberately compressed and overstated to provoke rather than persuade. Mastery here means engaging seriously with an aphorism (arguing for and against it using your own experience as evidence), not agreeing or disagreeing reflexively.

title: Ergodicity & the time average
book: Skin in the Game / "The Logic of Risk Taking"
grounding: Ensemble average: averaging across many people/parallel trials at one moment (100 people gambling once). Time average: one person's own sequential path through repeated exposure (one person gambling 100 times). A system is ergodic when these converge; they diverge wherever there's a real chance of irreversible ruin. What looks like risk aversion is often a correct read of ergodicity, not a psychological quirk: a rational person facing a repeated, ruin-possible bet should refuse it because they only get one sequential life, not an ensemble to average across.

title: Platonicity
grounding: Quote: 'Platonicity: the focus on those pure, well-defined, and easily discernible objects like triangles, or more social notions like friendship or love, at the cost of ignoring those objects of seemingly messier and less tractable structures.' The ludic fallacy is the Platonic fallacy applied specifically to uncertainty. The Platonic fold is the point where a clean model touches messy reality and its side effects start to matter. Confirmation error (Platonic confirmation): looking for instances that confirm your model, and finding them.

title: Epistemic arrogance
grounding: Quote: 'Epistemic arrogance: Measure the difference between what someone actually knows and how much he thinks he knows. An excess will imply arrogance, a deficit humility. An epistemocrat is someone of epistemic humility, who holds his own knowledge in greatest suspicion.' This is a gap, not an absolute level — a genuine expert can still be epistemically arrogant if confidence outgrows even a large knowledge base.

title: The empty-suit problem
grounding: Quote: 'Empty-suit problem (or "expert problem"): Some professionals have no differential abilities from the rest of the population, but for some reason, and against their empirical records, are believed to be experts... They dress up their expertise in beautiful language, jargon, mathematics, and often wear expensive suits.' Distinct from the Intellectual Yet Idiot: this is about whether a field's confident output has ever actually beaten chance, regardless of who bears the cost of being wrong.

title: The round-trip fallacy
grounding: Quote: 'Round-trip fallacy: the confusion of absence of evidence of Black Swans (or something else) for evidence of absence of Black Swans (or something else).' 'No evidence of X' is a statement about the limits of your search; 'evidence X doesn't exist' is a much stronger claim about the world. The conversion from the first to the second tends to happen exactly in domains (rare, high-impact events) where data was least likely to have caught it.

title: Future blindness
grounding: Quote: 'Future blindness: our natural inability to take into account the properties of the future — like autism, which prevents one from taking into account the existence of the minds of others.' Not a knowledge gap closable by more information — a structural inability to hold a genuinely different future as a live possibility while planning, analogous to lacking a theory-of-mind faculty rather than lacking specific facts about other minds.

title: Locke's madman
grounding: Quote: 'Locke's madman: someone who makes impeccable and rigorous reasoning from faulty premises — such as Paul Samuelson, Robert Merton the minor, and Gerard Debreu — thus producing phony models of uncertainty that make us vulnerable to Black Swans.' Skill at valid reasoning and the truth of the premise being reasoned from are separate axes; rigorous mathematics applied to a false premise (e.g. that markets are Gaussian/Mediocristan) is more dangerous than sloppy reasoning, because the rigor suppresses scrutiny of the premise.

title: The reverse-engineering problem
grounding: Quote: 'It is easier to predict how an ice cube would melt into a puddle than, looking at a puddle, to guess the shape of the ice cube that may have caused it. This "inverse problem" makes narrative disciplines and accounts (such as histories) suspicious.' Forward prediction (cause to effect) is often well-posed; backward inference (effect to cause) is frequently underdetermined — many different causes could produce the identical observed outcome, so a single confident causal story is a guess, not a deduction.

title: Scorn of the abstract
grounding: Quote: 'Scorn of the abstract: favoring contextualized thinking over more abstract, though more relevant, matters.' We reason sharply and emotionally about one concrete, identifiable case, and go numb as stakes scale up into an abstraction with no face attached — even though the abstract, larger-scale case is often the more consequential one. (Note: the glossary's illustrative line about one death vs. a million is commonly but unreliably attributed to Stalin — cite the bias itself, not that attribution.)
